summary: Gruber makes the case for independent web publishing as superior to corporate media, using his own site Daring Fireball as exhibit A. He argues that corporate media sites sacrifice design, readability, and content quality in pursuit of ad-driven revenue, while independents can focus on quality because they answer only to readers. He introduces Google AdSense as a less invasive funding mechanism for independent publishers, contrasting it with the animated banner ads and bloated HTML of corporate sites like Business Week. The post also dissects how impression-based advertising incentivizes sensationalism over substance, and explains his plan to fund Daring Fireball through a combination of AdSense clicks and voluntary reader donations.
key_points: The hairpiece analogy illustrates the fundamental tension in designer-client relationships: clients pay for more, but more undermines quality — directly applicable to web design where less markup, fewer graphics, and omitting needless words produces better sites.

Corporate media web sites are objectively worse than independent ones: bloated code, animated ads, unreadable layouts, and invalid HTML — yet they're the ones getting paid, while independents work for free.
Impression-based advertising creates perverse incentives toward sensationalism, rewarding controversial clickbait over insightful analysis, as exemplified by columnists like Dvorak and Haddad.

Google AdSense represents a fundamentally better advertising model for independents: text-only, pay-per-click, no pop-ups, and an order of magnitude more revenue per reader than traditional banner ads.

The web is the only medium where independents consistently produce superior work to corporations, because the barriers to entry are negligible — a text editor and Apache server put you on equal footing with anyone.
money_quotes: Less markup. Less scripting. Fewer navigation elements. Fewer colors. Fewer graphics. Omit needless words. This is how you make a good web site. I know this for a fact.
A good web site costs a lot of money, and for a lot of money, most clients want a lot of web site.
My goal for Daring Fireball is not to attract the largest possible audience. Rather, I'm trying to attract the smartest possible audience.
The distinction worth making is not between professional vs. amateur web sites. That just means whether you're getting paid. The distinction worth making is corporate vs. independent.
Independent web publishers — individuals and small teams — have never considered the web, as a medium, to be a failure. On the contrary, they view it as a roaring success.
With a text editor and an Apache web server, you're on equal footing with any web site in the world.
The lone natural resource in the media landscape is attention.
Any traffic is good traffic when you're charging advertisers per impression.
key_insight: The web is the one medium where independent publishers can outperform corporate media on quality, and Google's pay-per-click advertising finally offers a funding model that doesn't require sacrificing that quality.
